lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — The 50S large-subunit inhibitors
Now to the 50S large subunit, where there are more classes and a recurring shared real estate problem that creates a specific kind of resistance.
Stay with me, because three of these drugs bind near the same spot, and that fact has clinical consequences.
First, the macrolides — erythromycin, azithromycin, clarithromycin.
The "-mycin" or "-thromycin" ending is your tag.
Macrolides bind the 50S subunit, specifically the 23S ribosomal RNA, near the exit tunnel — the channel through which the growing protein chain threads its way out of the ribosome.
By parking near that tunnel, the macrolide blocks the elongating peptide from getting out, and it interferes with translocation, the shifting step.
Protein synthesis stalls.
Generally bacteriostatic.
Macrolides are mainstays for respiratory infections, atypical pneumonia, and they're a common go-to for patients with penicillin allergy.

A mechanism-adjacent clinical note: macrolides, especially erythromycin and clarithromycin, inhibit a liver enzyme system, cytochrome P450, which means they interfere with the metabolism of many other drugs — so they're famous for drug-drug interactions.
That's not a ribosome effect; it's an off-target effect on your liver enzymes, but it's clinically huge, so I flag it.
Second, chloramphenicol.
This one binds the 50S subunit and directly inhibits the peptidyl transferase activity — it blocks the actual peptide-bond-forming reaction itself, the catalytic heart of the large subunit.
Very broad spectrum, bacteriostatic.
So why don't we use this wonderful broad-spectrum drug all the time?
Because of toxicity, and the toxicity is, once again, a selectivity story.
Chloramphenicol can suppress the bone marrow.
There's a dose-related, reversible marrow suppression that traces back to those mitochondrial ribosomes — the marrow cells are metabolically demanding and vulnerable.
And there's a rarer, idiosyncratic, catastrophic aplastic anemia.
Because of that toxicity, in wealthy healthcare systems chloramphenicol is now a rarely-used reserve agent — though it remains valuable globally because it's cheap and broad.
The lesson stands: target the 70S ribosome, risk the mitochondria, pay in toxicity.
Third, clindamycin, a lincosamide.
It binds the 50S subunit very close to where the macrolides bind and also interferes with peptide bond formation and the early elongation steps.

Clindamycin is especially useful against anaerobes and certain gram-positive infections, and it has a particular trick: because it shuts down protein synthesis, it shuts down production of the toxins that some bacteria secrete — so it's valuable in toxin-driven diseases even beyond simple killing.

Its signature adverse effect is that, as a fairly broad agent, it disrupts the normal gut flora and is classically associated with C. difficile colitis — that overgrowth complication I mentioned when we discussed spectrum.
Now here's the payoff for grouping these three together.
Macrolides, clindamycin, and a third class called streptogramins all bind the same general region of the 50S subunit.
That shared address creates a single resistance mechanism that knocks out all of them at once.
Bacteria can produce an enzyme — encoded by erm genes — that methylates the 23S ribosomal RNA right at the binding site.
One little methyl group added to the ribosomal RNA changes the shape of the shared binding pocket, and now the macrolide, the clindamycin, and the streptogramin all lose their grip simultaneously.

This is called MLS-B resistance — for Macrolide, Lincosamide, Streptogramin-B — and it's a beautiful, slightly scary example of how a single small modification to the target, costing the bacterium almost nothing, can defeat three entire drug classes that happen to share a parking spot.
Target modification again, but now with collateral breadth.
Fourth, a newer and important class: the oxazolidinones, the prototype being linezolid.

These also bind the 50S subunit, on the 23S RNA, but at a distinct site, and their mechanism is to block the very first step — they prevent assembly of the functional initiation complex, the joining of the subunits and the first tRNA that has to happen before synthesis can even begin.

Because they hit a unique site by a unique mechanism, there's no cross-resistance with the older classes — that's exactly why linezolid is valuable: it works against resistant gram-positives including MRSA and VRE.
Generally bacteriostatic.

And the toxicities, predictably, trace to the mitochondrial ribosome again with longer use — marrow suppression and nerve effects — plus linezolid happens to inhibit an enzyme called monoamine oxidase, which creates some drug and food interactions.
New drug, same underlying selectivity tradeoff.
So step back and look at the whole ribosome category.

Two subunits, a handful of classes, each jamming a specific step: aminoglycosides cause misreading and are cidal; tetracyclines block A-site docking and are static; macrolides and clindamycin and chloramphenicol jam elongation and bond formation on the 50S and are mostly static; linezolid blocks initiation.
And running underneath all of them is the same toxicity logic — the closer the bacterial 70S resembles your mitochondrial ribosomes, the more you pay.
That's the unifying story of target two.
